titleStmt.title: Letter from William Tyrrell Jr. to Elizabeth Tyrrell, 22 August 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Tyrrell Jr.

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
The letters of William Upton Tyrrell ('Willie') to his father William J.H. Tyrrell, mother Elizabeth, sister Minnie and brother Robert are a notable example of wartime correspondence. The letters - nearly 200 in total - are mostly written by William to his family while in active service with the Royal Irish Rifles in France. Willie discusses his life and the lives of other soldiers in the trenches, as well as life at Ballindoolin, the family estate.
The family name 'Tyrrell' is sometimes misspelled 'Tyrell' in correspondence to the family.
Summary of the present letter:
Letter from Willie to his mother. William requests new underwear as his are full of holes.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
22nd August 1917 My dearest Mamy When I write to you so often you
cannot expect long letters + what is
more if you did expect them you
would not get them as there is absolutely
not a word of news. I discovered today much to my distress
that all my cotton drawers are almost
non-existent + I am not sufficient
of a tailor to make them return to their
former state so if there are any old
ones b about that are not all in holes
please send them on to me. That is
really the only exciting news. I had
a long letter yesterday from Mr. B
+ also one from Daddy which I was
delighted to get. I am very sorry you
are getting flooded out now. It is just
what we got the first week of August. Out
here the weather has quite reformed and
is much too hot now. No news but will write
again tomorrow probably. With love from Your most eff Son,
Willie
